methods.method_name: Finite Element Simulation of Tooth Compression
methods.description: This method describes the process of creating geometric models of black drum teeth based on imaging data and simulating compression tests using the finite element method (FEM) in ABAQUS. It details the simplification of the 3D problem to a 2D axisymmetric problem, the material modeling (three phases: enameloid, dentin, toothplate), material properties acquisition, boundary conditions (rigid compression plate, frictionless contact), simulation execution, and mesh convergence analysis.

steps.description: The geometric models of the black drum teeth were generated based on the manual tracing and simplification of the SEM and μCT projection images.
steps.searchable_summary: generate geometric models from SEM and μCT images

steps.description: The FE simulation of the compression tests on an individual tooth (with/without toothplate) was simplified as a 2D problem, where a 360°-rotation can construct the 2D simulation into a 3D FE compression.
steps.searchable_summary: simplify 3D tooth model to 2D axisymmetric model for FEA

steps.description: The tooth was modeled as a three-phase composite, which consists of an outer enameloid, an underlying dentin, and a supportive bony toothplate, where the contact between the phases is considered as perfectly bonded.
steps.searchable_summary: model tooth as a three-phase composite with perfect bonding
outputs: FE model with defined material phases (enameloid, dentin, toothplate)

steps.description: All three phases were modeled as isotropic, elastic-perfectly plastic using four-node bilinear axisymmetric quadrilateral elements (CAX4R in ABAQUS element library).
steps.searchable_summary: define material models (isotropic, elastic-perfectly plastic) and element types (CAX4R)
inputs: FE model with defined material phases
outputs: FE model with material models and element types assigned

steps.description: The material properties of the enameloid and dentin were obtained from Berkovich nanoindenation experiments where σ y ≈ H/2.8 [33]
steps.searchable_summary: calculate yield strength for enameloid and dentin from nanoindentation hardness

steps.description: and the material properties of the bone were taken from literature [34].
steps.searchable_summary: obtain bone material properties from literature
outputs: Elastic-perfectly plastic material properties for bone

steps.description: In addition, the compression tests were simulated by applying the loading through the upper rigid-body compression plate, where large-deformation theory and frictionless contact between the plates and teeth samples were assumed.
steps.searchable_summary: apply compression loading via rigid plate and define contact conditions
outputs: FE model with boundary conditions (loading, contact)

steps.description: where large-deformation theory and frictionless contact between the plates and teeth samples were assumed.
steps.searchable_summary: specify large-deformation theory for simulation
outputs: FE model with large-deformation theory enabled

steps.description: The simulation was performed in ABAQUS with the general statics modulus.
steps.searchable_summary: run finite element simulation in ABAQUS using general statics
inputs: Complete FE model (geometry, materials, BCs, settings)
outputs: FE simulation results (displacements, stresses, strains, reaction forces)

steps.description: Mesh convergence was also performed for accurate solutions to the load-displacement curves and stress contours.
steps.searchable_summary: perform mesh convergence analysis
inputs: FE simulation results from multiple mesh densities

steps.description: for accurate solutions to the load-displacement curves and stress contours.
steps.searchable_summary: extract load-displacement curves and stress contours from simulation results

methods.method_name: Correlative Raman Spectroscopy and EDS Analysis
methods.description: This method describes the use of correlative Raman Spectroscopy (RS) and Energy-dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDS) to investigate the chemical composition and structure of black drum teeth. It details RS mapping, spectral analysis (peak position, FWHM), identification of mineral and organic phases, and EDS measurement for elemental composition and gradients.

steps.description: The chemical information of the black drum teeth was investigated through correlative RS and EDS analyses (Fig. 3).
steps.searchable_summary: perform correlative Raman Spectroscopy and EDS analysis on tooth samples

steps.description: A high-resolution RS map across the entire tooth thickness direction (yellow box, Fig. 3A) ... reveal that both enameloid and dentin regions have a phosphate ν 1 peak at 959-964 cm -1
steps.searchable_summary: acquire high-resolution Raman spectroscopy map across tooth thickness

steps.description: ...and the averaged Raman spectra (Fig. 3B) reveal that both enameloid and dentin regions have a phosphate ν 1 peak at 959-964 cm -1
steps.searchable_summary: obtain and average Raman spectra for enameloid and dentin regions

steps.description: ...indicating that the main mineral phase in both regions is similar to hydroxyapatite (HA, standard peak position ca. 961 cm -1 ) [40].
steps.searchable_summary: analyze phosphate ν1 peak position to identify mineral phase

steps.description: However, the peak position shifts to higher wavenumbers in enameloid (962-964 cm -1 ) in comparison to that in dentin (959-961 cm -1 ) (Fig. 3C), which is due to the different chemical environment in the two regions.
steps.searchable_summary: compare phosphate peak positions between enameloid and dentin
inputs: Phosphate ν1 peak positions for enameloid
Phosphate ν1 peak positions for dentin

steps.description: Quantitative analysis of the width (full width at half maximum, FWHM) of this peak further reveals a broadening effect in the dentin region (Fig. 3D), suggesting its lower crystallinity [41].
steps.searchable_summary: quantify phosphate peak width (FWHM) to assess crystallinity
outputs: Full width at half maximum (FWHM) values

steps.description: In addition, the remaining evident peaks in the dentin region indicate the presence of a higher amount of proteinaceous materials (Amide III 1200-1300 cm -1 , Amide I 1660-1670 cm -1 , and C-H 2850-3300 cm -1 , Fig. 3B) [42][43][44]
steps.searchable_summary: identify proteinaceous material peaks in dentin Raman spectra
outputs: Identification of Amide III, Amide I, and C-H peaks

steps.description: Strong fluorescence was observed when conducting RS measurements on immature teeth, due to their higher concentration of organic materials.
steps.searchable_summary: note observation of strong fluorescence during RS on immature teeth

steps.description: Correlative EDS measurement performed on the same sample reveals the major chemical elements and their compositional gradients within the mature teeth ( Fig. 3E-H).
steps.searchable_summary: perform correlative EDS measurement on the same tooth sample
inputs: Mature tooth sample (previously used for RS)

steps.description: Fig. 3E summarizes the averaged EDS spectra of the enameloid (blue) and dentin (red)
steps.searchable_summary: obtain and average EDS spectra for enameloid and dentin regions

steps.description: where the distribution of the major elements Ca, F, Mg, and Zn is shown in Fig. 3F. ... First, Ca and P are distributed across the entire tooth (Fig. 3G)... Third, the trace elements F and Zn are mainly present in the enameloid layer... while Mg shows a higher concentration in the dentin layer...
steps.searchable_summary: analyze distribution and gradients of major and trace elements (Ca, P, F, Mg, Zn) using EDS maps
outputs: Elemental distribution maps (Ca, P, F, Mg, Zn)

steps.description: Table S5 quantifies and compares the EDS results (non-carbon elements) with the theoretical values of HA and FA (fluorapatite)
steps.searchable_summary: quantify EDS results for non-carbon elements

steps.description: Table S5 quantifies and compares the EDS results (non-carbon elements) with the theoretical values of HA and FA (fluorapatite)
steps.searchable_summary: compare quantitative EDS results with theoretical values of hydroxyapatite and fluorapatite
outputs: Comparison data between EDS and theoretical compositions

steps.description: from which the mineral phase in the enameloid is estimated to be Ca 10 (PO 4 ) 6 (OH) 0.89 F 1.11 with Zn substitutions (Supplementary Note 2), while the dentin has a mineral phase more similar to HA with Mg substitutions.
steps.searchable_summary: estimate mineral phase composition based on quantitative EDS comparison
inputs: Comparison data between EDS and theoretical compositions
outputs: Estimated mineral phase composition for enameloid (e.g., Ca10(PO4)6(OH)0.89F1.11 with Zn)
Estimated mineral phase composition for dentin (e.g., HA with Mg substitutions)

steps.description: These conclusions agree well with the RS measurement: the Zn-doped HA/FA crystals in the enameloid layer contribute to the peak shift to higher wavenumbers [40], while Mg substitution in dentin results in the peak shift to lower wavenumbers [48].
steps.searchable_summary: correlate RS peak shifts with elemental substitutions identified by EDS
inputs: Estimated mineral phase composition from EDS
outputs: Integrated conclusions correlating RS and EDS findings

methods.description: This method describes the use of Polarization-dependent imaging contrast (PIC) mapping techniques to determine the crystallographic orientations of enameloid crystals based on dichroism (spectral peak intensity variation with orientation).

steps.description: Polarization-dependent imaging contrast (PIC) mapping techniques were employed to determine the crystallographic orientations of the enameloid crystals.
steps.searchable_summary: perform Polarization-dependent imaging contrast (PIC) mapping

steps.description: Many anisotropic crystals exhibit dichroism, i.e., spectral peak intensity variations depending on the crystallographic orientations; such dichroism can therefore be used in PIC maps to measure and display crystal orientation [38].
steps.searchable_summary: measure spectral peak intensity variations (dichroism) from PIC map data

steps.description: such dichroism can therefore be used in PIC maps to measure and display crystal orientation [38].
steps.searchable_summary: use dichroism measurements to determine and display crystal orientation

methods.description: This method describes experimental compression tests conducted on individual mature black drum teeth, both with and without bony toothplates, to investigate their macroscopic mechanical behavior and failure modes.

steps.description: To investigate the teeth's macroscopic mechanical behavior, compression tests were conducted on individual mature teeth with and without bony toothplates ( Fig. 7A-C).
steps.searchable_summary: conduct compression tests on individual mature teeth with and without toothplates
inputs: Mature black drum teeth (with bony toothplates)
Mature black drum teeth (without bony toothplates)

steps.description: The failure loads for individual teeth range between 150 and 600 N depending on the sizes (Fig. S9).
steps.searchable_summary: measure and record failure loads from compression tests

steps.description: The representative nominal stressstrain curves for the two groups of samples are shown in Fig. 7A.
steps.searchable_summary: calculate and plot nominal stress-strain curves from force-displacement data

steps.description: Typically, the teeth with bony toothplates experience three distinct deformation stages: toothplate deformation and failure, tooth repositioning, and tooth deformation and failure (Fig. 7A,B).
steps.searchable_summary: observe and describe deformation stages for teeth with toothplates during compression
inputs: Compression test on teeth with toothplates
outputs: Description of deformation stages (toothplate deformation/failure, repositioning, tooth deformation/failure)

steps.description: In contrast, the detailed deformation process of the teeth is revealed by the tests for samples without bony toothplates, which involves initial fracture via vertical cracking ( Fig. 7C-i), horizontal cracks connecting adjacent vertical cracks ( Fig. 7C-ii), and final collapse ( Fig. 7C-iii).
steps.searchable_summary: observe and describe deformation process and failure modes for teeth without toothplates during compression
inputs: Compression test on teeth without toothplates
outputs: Description of deformation process (vertical cracking, horizontal cracking, collapse)
